front: Empire: ruled from its capital at Gao on the Niger River
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Empire: succeeded the Mali Empire as the dominant trade power of the Sahel
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Empire: derives its name from its ruling ethnic group
work: General / Giveaway Identifiers

front: Ruler: founder of the Songhai Empire who defeated the Dogon and Fulani peoples near the modern-day city of Bandiagara
back: Sunni Ali / Sonni Ali Ber (Songhai Empire)
work: Founding under Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber)

front: Ruler: expelled the Tuareg from Timbuktu
back: Sunni Ali / Sonni Ali Ber (Songhai Empire)
work: Founding under Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber)

front: Ruler: earned the epithet "merciless" for his treatment of the Fulbe (Fulani) people
back: Sunni Ali / Sonni Ali Ber (Songhai Empire)
work: Founding under Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber)

front: Ruler: legendarily claimed he could transform himself into a vulture and make his armies invisible
back: Sunni Ali / Sonni Ali Ber (Songhai Empire)
work: Founding under Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber)

front: Ruler: conquered the remnants of the Mali Empire and gained wealth through control of trade cities like Timbuktu
back: Sunni Ali / Sonni Ali Ber (Songhai Empire)
work: Founding under Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber)

front: Event: the Songhai ethnic group had already inhabited the future capital, Gao, since the earlier reign of a King Kossoi
back: Founding of Gao / pre-imperial Songhai (Songhai Empire)
work: Founding under Sunni Ali (Sonni Ali Ber)

front: Ruler: seized the throne from Sunni Ali's son Sonni Baru at the Battle of Anfao, assisted by his brother Kanfari Omar
work: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire

front: Ruler: founded the Askia dynasty, replacing the Sonni dynasty
work: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire

front: Ruler: the Songhai Empire reached its greatest territorial extent under his reign
work: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire

front: Ruler: buried beneath a mud-brick tomb in Gao whose protruding wooden scaffolding gives it the appearance of a spiky pyramid
work: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire

front: Ruler: was advised on religious and occult matters by the scholar al-Maghili, in a set of writings called "al-Maghili's Answers"
work: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire

front: Ruler: during a debt-ridden hajj to Mecca, waged campaigns against the Mossi and conquered the Sultanate of Agadez
work: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire

front: Ruler: his hajj account plagiarized the earlier record of Mansa Musa's pilgrimage
work: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire

front: Ruler: established qadis (judges) in every Songhai city
work: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire

front: Ruler: his daughter Awah married Muhammad Rumfa, making him an ancestor of the sultans of Kano
work: Askia Muhammad I & the Height of the Empire

front: Institution: contested the salt mines of Taghaza with Morocco, fought over by the ruler Dawud
work: Administration, Trade & Chronicles

front: Document: chronicled the empire's history in the Tarikh al-fattash, attributed to the scribe Mahmud Kati and commissioned by ruler Dawud
work: Administration, Trade & Chronicles

front: Institution: relied on hikoi (river boats) to manage its Niger river fleet, combined with cavalry, for most of its military power
work: Administration, Trade & Chronicles

front: Document: a surviving copy of the empire's history, "Manuscript C," was argued by historian Nehemia Levtzion to have been forged by Shehu Amadu
work: Administration, Trade & Chronicles

front: Event: a history of the empire contains a prophecy declaring the founder of the Massina state to be the Mahdi
work: Administration, Trade & Chronicles

front: Event: a Songhai ruler who captured the city of Djenne persecuted the scholars of the University of Sankore
work: Administration, Trade & Chronicles

front: Figure: this scholar was forced into exile during the Moroccan invasion of the Songhai Empire
work: Administration, Trade & Chronicles

front: Battle: the 1591 defeat of Songhai forces, led by the last ruler Askia Ishaq II, by Moroccan forces
work: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)

front: Battle: the sound of Moroccan cannons and arquebuses turned back a Songhai cattle stampede meant to break the Moroccan lines
work: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)

front: Event: ordered by Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur of Morocco, nicknamed al-Dhahabi ("the Golden") for the campaign's short-term gains, seeking control of the gold-salt trade
back: Moroccan invasion of the Songhai Empire
work: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)

front: Figure: this eunuch general destroyed the salt mines at Taghaza before winning the Battle of Tondibi and sacking Djenne, Gao, and Timbuktu
work: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)

front: Event: after the collapse, a prince of the last royal Askia dynasty, Askia Nuhu, founded the successor Dendi Kingdom
work: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)

front: Event: the Moroccan invasion was preceded a decade earlier by their conquest of the Tuwat and Gurara oases
back: Moroccan invasion of the Songhai Empire
work: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)

front: Event: manpower for the Moroccan campaign came partly from captives taken at the Battle of Alcacer Quibir
back: Moroccan invasion of the Songhai Empire
work: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)

front: Figure: victorious Moroccan military elites intermarried and settled, forming this ethnic group named from the Arabic word for "fusilier"
work: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)

front: Document: the death of the empire's final ruler, in retribution for the death of a company of fusiliers, is chronicled here
work: Fall: The Moroccan Invasion & the Battle of Tondibi (1591)
